what-claude-can-do.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to help you with a specific work task. Be concrete — mention your role and what you need.
what-claude-can-do.placeholder: e.g., I'm a marketing manager. Help me...
what-claude-can-do.hint: I'm a project manager. Write a status update email for my team about our Q2 product launch being on track, with 3 key milestones completed.
what-claude-can-do.goodExample: I'm a marketing manager. Draft a 200-word LinkedIn post announcing our new AI-powered analytics tool, targeting small business owners. Tone: professional but approachable.
what-claude-can-do.successMessage: Great start! You included your role and a specific task — that's exactly how to get useful output from Claude.

what-claude-can-do.instruction: Now try asking Claude a question about something you're curious about. Include context about WHY you want to know.
what-claude-can-do.placeholder: e.g., I'm learning about... Can you explain...
what-claude-can-do.hint: I'm new to investing and confused by all the jargon. Can you explain what an ETF is in simple terms, with a real-world analogy?
what-claude-can-do.successMessage: Nice! Adding context about why you're asking helps Claude tailor the explanation to your level.

your-first-conversation.instruction: Write a prompt that includes three things: (1) your role, (2) the task, and (3) the desired format.
your-first-conversation.hint: As a freelance writer, create a 5-point outline for a blog post about remote work productivity tips. Use bullet points.
your-first-conversation.goodExample: As a freelance writer, create a 5-point outline for a blog post about remote work productivity tips. Use bullet points with a one-sentence explanation for each.
your-first-conversation.successMessage: That's the formula: Role + Task + Format. This structure consistently gets better outputs from Claude.

your-first-conversation.instruction: Rewrite this vague prompt to make it specific: 'Write me an email'
your-first-conversation.hint: Write a professional follow-up email to a client named Sarah who hasn't responded to our proposal in 2 weeks. Keep it friendly and under 150 words.
your-first-conversation.goodExample: Write a professional follow-up email to a client who hasn't responded to our project proposal in 2 weeks. Tone: warm but direct. Include a soft deadline. Under 150 words.
your-first-conversation.successMessage: See the difference? Specificity transforms a useless prompt into one that gets you exactly what you need.

your-first-conversation.instruction: Write a prompt that tells Claude what NOT to do. Constraints are just as important as instructions.
your-first-conversation.hint: Explain blockchain to a 10-year-old. Don't use technical jargon. Don't mention cryptocurrency. Use a fun analogy involving a school lunchroom.
your-first-conversation.successMessage: Constraints are a superpower. Telling Claude what to avoid is often more impactful than telling it what to include.

custom-instructions.instruction: Write a system prompt that defines Claude's role and communication style for a specific use case.
custom-instructions.hint: You are a friendly fitness coach who specializes in beginners. Always suggest modifications for exercises. Never recommend extreme diets. Keep responses encouraging and under 200 words.
custom-instructions.goodExample: You are a patient coding tutor for Python beginners. Always explain concepts with real-world analogies before showing code. Never assume prior programming knowledge. When showing code, include comments explaining each line. Keep a warm, encouraging tone.
custom-instructions.successMessage: That's a solid system prompt! It gives Claude a clear identity, communication rules, and boundaries.

custom-instructions.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to adopt a specific tone. Try: formal, casual, sarcastic, motivational, or academic.
custom-instructions.hint: Write a motivational Monday morning message for a sales team that just had a tough quarter. Be real about the challenges but end on an energizing note. Sound like a coach, not a corporate memo.
custom-instructions.successMessage: Tone control is underrated. The same information delivered in the right voice makes all the difference.

documents-and-data.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to analyze or summarize information. Specify what to focus on and how to present the results.
documents-and-data.hint: Summarize the key findings from this quarterly report. Focus on revenue trends and customer churn. Present as 5 bullet points, each under 20 words. Flag any numbers that changed more than 15% from last quarter.
documents-and-data.goodExample: Analyze this meeting transcript and extract: (1) all action items with owners, (2) key decisions made, (3) unresolved questions. Format as a table with columns: Item, Owner, Deadline, Status.
documents-and-data.successMessage: Telling Claude exactly what to extract and how to format it turns a wall of text into actionable intelligence.

documents-and-data.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to transform data from one format to another.
documents-and-data.hint: Take these meeting notes in paragraph form and convert them into a structured action items list. Each item should have: task, responsible person, and deadline. Sort by deadline, soonest first.
documents-and-data.successMessage: Data transformation is one of Claude's most practical superpowers. You'll use this daily.

email-calendar-magic.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to draft an email. Include: recipient context, purpose, tone, and length constraint.
email-calendar-magic.hint: Draft a polite but firm email to a vendor who delivered our order 2 weeks late. Acknowledge the relationship, express the impact on our operations, and request a 10% discount on the next order. Professional tone. Under 200 words.
email-calendar-magic.badExample: Write an email to my boss
email-calendar-magic.goodExample: Draft a thank-you email to my manager Lisa for approving my conference attendance. Mention I'm excited about the AI track specifically. Keep it brief (3-4 sentences), professional but warm. Don't be overly formal.
email-calendar-magic.successMessage: Context + purpose + tone + length = email prompts that save you 15 minutes per email.

email-calendar-magic.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to help you prepare for a meeting. Include what you need and the context.
email-calendar-magic.hint: I have a 30-minute meeting with a potential client tomorrow. They run a small bakery chain (5 locations) and want to use AI to optimize their supply ordering. Create 5 discovery questions I should ask, plus 3 talking points about how AI can help their specific industry.
email-calendar-magic.successMessage: Meeting prep is a killer use case. Claude can generate questions, talking points, and briefing docs in seconds.

build-your-assistant.instruction: Design a prompt template you could reuse every day for a repetitive work task. Include placeholders for variable information.
build-your-assistant.hint: Create a daily standup update template: 'Yesterday I completed [TASKS]. Today I'm working on [PRIORITIES]. I'm blocked on [BLOCKERS]. My confidence level for hitting this week's goals: [HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW] because [REASON].'
build-your-assistant.badExample: Help me with my daily work
build-your-assistant.goodExample: Here's my weekly report template. Fill it in based on the notes I provide: 'This week: [3 accomplishments]. Next week: [3 priorities]. Risk: [any blockers]. Mood: [one emoji].' Keep each item to one sentence.
build-your-assistant.successMessage: Templates are the gateway to automation. Save this pattern — you'll use it constantly.

build-your-assistant.instruction: Write a multi-step prompt — ask Claude to do something, then refine or build on the result.
build-your-assistant.hint: First, brainstorm 10 blog post titles about AI productivity tips. Then, pick the 3 most clickable titles and write a one-paragraph outline for each. Finally, draft the opening paragraph for the best one.
build-your-assistant.successMessage: Multi-step prompts unlock Claude's full power. Each step builds on the last, creating a workflow in one conversation.
